description: Scheduled transport of passengers (and cargo) on aircraft over a network of routes. Levers are capacity deployment (seats x distance flown), demand capture (load factor, yield), operational reliability (on-time, cancellations), and asset/cost efficiency (utilization, unit cost per seat-mile).

metrics.name: Load Factor
metrics.definition: Share of available seat capacity that was actually sold/occupied, measured in distance-weighted seat-miles (or seat-kilometers).
metrics.formula: SUM(RPK) / NULLIF(SUM(ASK), 0)  -- equivalently SUM(passengers * distance) / SUM(seats_available * distance). Only when every flight is the same stage length does it reduce to SUM(passengers) / SUM(seats).
metrics.grain: Flight (or flight-leg) grain, then capacity-weight. Compute RPK and ASK per leg, SUM each across the population, THEN divide. This weights long/high-capacity flights correctly. Never average per-flight load factors.
anti_patterns: AVG(passengers / seats) over flights gives an unweighted mean — a 50-seat regional jet and a 400-seat widebody count equally, badly skewing the network number. Always SUM(RPK)/SUM(ASK).
Using seats SOLD as the denominator instead of seats AVAILABLE yields ~100% always; the denominator is capacity, not bookings.

A result > 1.0 (>100%) means a join fanned passengers out (e.g. legs joined to segments, or bookings double-counted), an overbooking artifact, or passengers counted against the wrong seat count — it is a bug, not a full plane.
metrics.sane_range: ratio 0..1 (0..100%); industry-typical 0.75–0.90. Any value > 1.0 is impossible and indicates a grain/join error.

metrics.name: Revenue Passenger Kilometers/Miles vs Available Seat Kilometers/Miles (RPK/ASK)
metrics.definition: RPK/RPM is traffic carried = passengers x distance flown. ASK/ASM is capacity offered = seats available x distance flown. Together they size demand against supply.
metrics.formula: RPK = SUM(passengers * stage_distance);  ASK = SUM(seats_available * stage_distance)  -- both summed at flight-leg grain over the period/route.
metrics.grain: Flight-leg grain. Compute the product (count x distance) PER leg first, then SUM. Distance is the actual stage length flown for that leg, not the route's nominal great-circle distance unless that's all you have.
anti_patterns: SUM(passengers) * SUM(distance) (summing each factor separately then multiplying) is wrong — the product must be formed per leg before summing, or you cross-multiply unrelated flights and explode the number.
Mixing miles and kilometers across legs without a unit conversion silently corrupts the total (1 mile ≈ 1.609 km).
Counting bookings/tickets (which can span multiple legs) as if each were one leg-passenger inflates RPK; explode multi-leg itineraries to legs first.
metrics.sane_range: RPK/ASK are large positive counts (units: passenger-km or seat-km/miles), scaling into billions for a full carrier annually. RPK must always be <= ASK for the same population (RPK > ASK implies load factor > 1, a bug).

metrics.name: On-Time Performance (OTP)
metrics.definition: Share of flights that arrive (or depart) within an on-time threshold, conventionally less than 15 minutes after scheduled time (the 'A14'/D0:15 standard).
metrics.formula: COUNT(flights WHERE arrival_delay_minutes < on_time_threshold) / NULLIF(COUNT(operated_flights), 0)  -- threshold conventionally 15 minutes.
metrics.grain: Flight (leg) grain, one row per operated flight. Denominator is OPERATED flights only — cancelled and diverted flights are excluded from OTP (they belong to cancellation/completion metrics), not counted as 'late'.
anti_patterns: Including cancelled flights in the denominator (or treating their NULL delay as 'on time') distorts OTP — cancellations are a separate metric; an airline could cancel its way to 100% OTP.
AVG(delay_minutes) is average delay, NOT on-time performance; OTP is a threshold COUNT ratio, not a mean.

Counting a flight late at <= 15 vs < 15, or measuring departure vs arrival delay inconsistently, shifts the number by points — fix the threshold and the delay basis (arrival is the standard) and apply uniformly.
Early arrivals have negative delay and ARE on time; treating ABS(delay) > 15 as late wrongly penalizes early flights.
needs_concepts: scheduled vs actual arrival time (delay minutes)

metrics.name: Cancellation Rate
metrics.definition: Share of scheduled flights that were cancelled (completion factor is its complement: flights operated / flights scheduled).
metrics.formula: COUNT(DISTINCT cancelled_flight) / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT scheduled_flight), 0)  -- completion_factor = 1 - cancellation_rate.
metrics.grain: Scheduled-flight grain. Denominator is flights SCHEDULED (the plan), numerator is the subset cancelled. A flight is one scheduled departure key (flight number + date + origin), counted once.
anti_patterns: Using OPERATED flights as the denominator excludes the cancelled flights themselves, understating the rate — the denominator must be everything scheduled.
Counting cancellation event rows (a flight can log multiple status updates) instead of DISTINCT cancelled flights inflates the numerator.
Diversions are not cancellations — don't lump them; a diverted flight still operated.
metrics.sane_range: ratio 0..1 (0..100%); industry-typical 0.005–0.03 (under 3%). Sustained values above ~0.1 signal an operational crisis or a counting error.

metrics.name: Yield (Passenger Revenue per RPK/RPM)
metrics.definition: Passenger revenue earned per unit of traffic carried — the price of a seat-mile actually sold.
metrics.formula: SUM(passenger_revenue) / NULLIF(SUM(RPK), 0)  -- where RPK = SUM(passengers * distance) at leg grain.
metrics.grain: Aggregate passenger revenue and RPK over the SAME population (route/period) at compatible grain, then divide. Revenue must be PASSENGER revenue only (exclude cargo and most ancillary, which distort the fare-per-mile signal).
anti_patterns: Dividing by ASK instead of RPK gives RASM (a capacity metric), not yield — yield is per TRAFFIC (RPK).
AVG(per-flight yield) is unweighted; weight by traffic via SUM(revenue)/SUM(RPK).
Including cargo or non-ticket revenue in the numerator overstates passenger yield; keep the numerator to passenger ticket revenue.
Mixing currencies across markets without FX conversion corrupts the aggregate.
metrics.sane_range: currency per km or per mile (small), e.g. ~0.05–0.20 USD per RPM; values near 1+ USD/mile or near-zero scientific-notation indicate a unit/grain mismatch.

metrics.name: RASM / CASM (Unit Revenue / Unit Cost per ASM)
metrics.definition: RASM is total operating revenue per unit of capacity (ASM/ASK); CASM is total operating cost per unit of capacity. Their spread is the airline's per-seat-mile profitability.
metrics.formula: RASM = SUM(operating_revenue) / NULLIF(SUM(ASM), 0);  CASM = SUM(operating_cost) / NULLIF(SUM(ASM), 0)  -- ASM = SUM(seats_available * distance) at leg grain.
metrics.grain: Aggregate revenue (or cost) and ASM over the SAME period/network at compatible grain, then divide. Unit metrics are CAPACITY-weighted because the denominator is ASM. CASM-ex excludes fuel and special items from the numerator — keep numerator scope consistent.
anti_patterns: Dividing by RPK instead of ASM conflates unit revenue with yield — RASM/CASM are per CAPACITY (ASM), not per traffic.
AVG(per-flight RASM) is unweighted and ignores stage length; use SUM(revenue)/SUM(ASM).
Comparing a carrier's RASM to another's CASM that includes/excludes fuel inconsistently is apples-to-oranges; align the cost scope (ex-fuel vs all-in).
Summing revenue at one grain and ASM at another (e.g. system revenue but only domestic ASM) yields an inflated unit figure.
metrics.sane_range: currency per ASM/ASK in cents, typically ~10–20 US cents per ASM for both RASM and CASM; a profitable carrier has RASM > CASM. Values like dollars-per-ASM or 1e-9 indicate a unit/grain error.

metrics.name: Ancillary Revenue per Passenger
metrics.definition: Non-ticket (bags, seat selection, priority, onboard, loyalty) revenue earned per passenger carried.
metrics.formula: SUM(ancillary_revenue) / NULLIF(COUNT(DISTINCT passenger), 0)  -- passengers carried, enplaned segment passengers.
metrics.grain: Passenger (enplanement) grain. Numerator is ancillary-only revenue (exclude base fare); denominator is passengers actually carried over the SAME period/network as the revenue.
anti_patterns: Dividing by BOOKINGS or itineraries instead of enplaned passengers mismatches the grain when itineraries carry multiple passengers/legs.
Including base ticket fare in the numerator turns this into total revenue per passenger, not ancillary.
Counting passengers from one period against ancillary revenue from another inflates/deflates the ratio — align the windows.
Double-counting passengers across legs of one itinerary shrinks the per-passenger figure; define the passenger population explicitly (enplanements vs unique travelers).
metrics.sane_range: currency per passenger, typically ~5–60 USD/pax (higher for LCCs/ULCCs). Negative or near-zero only on a revenue-mapping error.

metrics.name: Aircraft / Fleet Utilization (Block Hours per Aircraft per Day)
metrics.definition: Average revenue-flying block hours (gate-out to gate-in) generated per aircraft per day — how hard the fleet's assets work.
metrics.formula: SUM(block_hours) / NULLIF((COUNT(DISTINCT aircraft) * days_in_period), 0)  -- block hours summed over the period, divided by aircraft-days available.
metrics.grain: Block-hours summed at flight-leg grain; denominator is aircraft-DAYS (distinct active aircraft x days in the period). Block time is gate-to-gate, not airborne 'flight time'.
anti_patterns: Dividing total block hours by COUNT(flights) gives hours-per-flight (stage time), NOT daily utilization per aircraft.
Counting aircraft from the whole fleet roster (including stored/parked tails) in the denominator understates utilization; count active aircraft, and pro-rate aircraft that entered/left mid-period.
Summing airborne/flight hours instead of BLOCK hours undercounts utilization (block includes taxi).
Using a single day's aircraft count x the full period instead of true aircraft-days when fleet size changes biases the result.
metrics.sane_range: hours per aircraft per day, 0..24 (physically capped at 24); industry-typical 8–14 (LCCs/narrowbodies higher, long-haul widebodies can exceed via long sectors). Any value > 24 is impossible.
